---
title: "From ask to full agentic systems"
description: "Consumers range from single-shot ask to multi-step plan/retrieve/act/update agents; the action primitive is what today's template is missing."
date: "2026-07-20"
categories: [research-agents]
draft: false
---

::: {.callout-note title="Status"}
**Provisional.** Explicitly forward-looking; the framing should evolve as concrete agent work produces evidence.
:::

**Thesis.** Agent systems are the *consumers* of the retrieval substrate. The vision provides the substrate; agents are the higher-order operators that call it.

At the light end is **ask**: a single shot. Extract seed concepts from the question, run a random walk over the wiki graph, take the top pages, synthesize an answer with citations. At the heavy end is a **full agent system**: plan, retrieve, act, and update the wiki as a side effect of working. The primitive that separates the two is **action**. Everything else, memory, retrieval, provenance, discipline, already exists in the template stack; the action primitive is what makes something an agent rather than a smart assistant with memory.

Three composition patterns fall out. Ask as a retrieval consumer. Wiki as agent memory, where the agent writes its findings and its log back as it goes. And corpus-scale retrieval, where an agent drops from the wiki down to the raw corpus when the wiki lacks coverage, then files a durable synthesis back up.

Three questions the vision does not yet answer. When should an agent trust the wiki versus fall back to the corpus (the cached-but-stale failure mode)? How does the action primitive interact with wiki correctness (provenance discipline matters *more* once an agent can write)? And what is the right ergonomic surface between the retrieval substrate and the orchestrator on top of it?

This one is deliberately provisional. The natural build progression looks like `/wiki-ask`, then `/wiki-verify`, then `/wiki-plan`, then multi-agent orchestration, but nothing on that list is committed to. The honest caution is the point: an agent that calls a bad retrieval tool still gets bad results. Agents raise the stakes for retrieval quality; they do not paper over retrieval limits.
